note_physics_1_1_0.js.title: Exam Tip: Always Convert to SI First
note_physics_1_1_0.js.text: Always check units in final answers. Unit algebra (e.g., dividing m by s gives m/s) is a key skill. Substituting non-SI units into equations often leads to incorrect answers — lost marks for forgetting unit conversion.

note_physics_1_1_0.js.title: Common Misconception: Prefixes
note_physics_1_1_0.js.text: Prefixes change the magnitude but not the base unit type. 1 mm is still a distance, not a different quantity. 1 mm = 10^−3 m, not 10^−3 something else. Students often confuse prefix notation with quantity changes.

note_physics_1_1_1.js.title: Common Mistake: Parallax Error
note_physics_1_1_1.js.text: Eyes must be perpendicular to the scale when reading analogue instruments. If viewing at an angle, the reading is distorted. Solution: use a mirror behind the scale — when your eye's reflection is hidden, you're reading perpendicular. Examiners reward students who mention this.

note_physics_1_1_1.js.title: Exam Tip: Zero Error Analysis
note_physics_1_1_1.js.text: Identify zero error (reading when instrument shows zero) and parallax error separately in your evaluation. Both are systematic errors requiring correction. Examiners specifically ask for these in practical write-ups.

note_physics_1_1_2.js.title: Exam Tip: Always Justify Estimates
note_physics_1_1_2.js.text: Show your breakdown into parts (e.g., for car mass: length × width × height × density). Examiners want to see your reasoning, not just a final number. A justified but incorrect estimate scores more marks than an unjustified correct one.

note_physics_1_1_2.js.title: Common Mistake: Mass vs Weight
note_physics_1_1_2.js.text: When estimating, always use mass (kg) for the quantity, not weight (N). A car's mass is ~1000 kg, not 1000 N. Weight depends on location; mass does not.

note_physics_1_1_3.js.title: Systematic Error: Zero Error
note_physics_1_1_3.js.text: A systematic error like zero error must be corrected. For a micrometer with zero error +0.05 mm: if you measure 5.45 mm, the true measurement is 5.45 − 0.05 = 5.40 mm. Failing to correct this biases all results.

note_physics_1_1_3.js.title: Exam Tip: Uncertainty Propagation
note_physics_1_1_3.js.text: When multiplying or dividing, add PERCENTAGE uncertainties, not absolute ones. Only switch to absolute uncertainties at the final step if reporting ± notation. This rule prevents underestimating uncertainty.

note_physics_1_1_4.js.title: Exam Tip: Significant Figures
note_physics_1_1_4.js.text: Quote results to 2-3 significant figures (not more). Too many sig figs suggests false precision. Graph axes must have quantity name AND unit. A conclusion must state the result with evidence and compare to a known value using percentage difference.

note_physics_1_1_4.js.title: Common Graph Mistake
note_physics_1_1_4.js.text: Plot data as crosses (not dots), draw a best-fit line (not joining dots), and include error bars. Missing any of these loses marks. Gradients calculated from two points far apart on the line reduce percentage error in coordinate reading.

note_physics_1_1_5.js.title: Exam Tip: Real-World Applications
note_physics_1_1_5.js.text: Medical: X-rays (ionising, radiation risk), MRI (safe, no ionising), ultrasound (safe, uses distance = speed × time). Engineering: Young modulus guides material selection. Communications: total internal reflection in fibres. Environment: solar, wind, nuclear all convert other forms to electrical.

note_physics_1_1_5.js.title: Applications and Risk-Benefit
note_physics_1_1_5.js.text: Every technology has risk-benefit trade-offs. X-rays: risk of radiation dose vs. diagnostic benefit. Nuclear: low-carbon but radioactive waste persistence. Examiners ask: 'Why is this technology beneficial despite risks?'

note_physics_1_1_6.js.title: Peer Review Limitations
note_physics_1_1_6.js.text: Peer review does not guarantee correctness. Reviewers can miss flaws, be biased, or have conflicts of interest. Novel findings can be rejected. Publication bias favours positive results over null results. Reproducibility crisis in psychology/medicine showed many published effects are not real.

note_physics_1_1_6.js.title: Exam Tip: Scientific Consensus
note_physics_1_1_6.js.text: Scientific knowledge develops through discovery → peer review → replication → consensus. No single experiment 'proves' something; repeated independent replication does. Media often overstates certainty compared to actual scientific consensus.

note_physics_1_1_7.js.title: Exam Tip: Risk-Benefit Framework
note_physics_1_1_7.js.text: Technology is approved when benefits (health, economy, knowledge, quality of life) outweigh risks (environmental, safety, ethical). Always frame decisions as balancing these factors. Example: nuclear power reduces carbon but leaves radioactive waste for millennia.

note_physics_1_1_7.js.title: Ethical Responsibilities in Science
note_physics_1_1_7.js.text: Scientists must report honestly even when results are commercially or politically inconvenient. Fraud destroys trust and wastes resources on false leads. Informed consent is required for human research. Long-term monitoring (CFC/ozone case study) is vital for detecting harm.

note_physics_1_2_0.js.title: Key Point: SUVAT Only for Constant Acceleration
note_physics_1_2_0.js.text: SUVAT equations apply ONLY when acceleration is constant. Air resistance violates this (a changes with speed). All five variables (s, u, v, a, t) must be in the same direction. Assign positive direction first; opposite motion is negative.

note_physics_1_2_0.js.title: Exam Tip: Terminal Velocity Misconception
note_physics_1_2_0.js.text: At terminal velocity: net force = 0 → acceleration = 0 → velocity is constant (NOT zero). The object is still moving at steady speed. When a parachutist opens the parachute, drag suddenly exceeds weight → net upward force → deceleration (not zero velocity).

note_physics_1_2_1.js.title: Common Error: Gradient vs Area Rules
note_physics_1_2_1.js.text: Students confuse gradient and area methods. Remember: gradient always moves forward (s→v→v→a). Area under s-t graph has NO simple physical meaning — never calculate it. Only area under v-t = displacement; area under a-t = change in velocity.

note_physics_1_2_1.js.title: Exam Tip: Calculating Gradient Accurately
note_physics_1_2_1.js.text: Choose two points far apart on the drawn line (NOT data points). The greater the separation, the smaller the percentage error in reading coordinates. Include units in the gradient (e.g., 2.5 m/s²).

note_physics_1_2_2.js.title: Common Mistake: Misinterpreting Areas
note_physics_1_2_2.js.text: Area under displacement-time graph is meaningless (not velocity, not anything physical). Area under velocity-time = displacement only. Area under acceleration-time = change in velocity only. Students often calculate the wrong area.

note_physics_1_2_2.js.title: Exam Tip: Tangent to Curved Graphs
note_physics_1_2_2.js.text: For a curved v-t graph, use a ruler to draw a tangent at the point of interest — the tangent must touch the curve at only that point, not crossing nearby. Extend the tangent line well across the graph so rise and run are large, reducing percentage error in gradient calculation.

note_physics_1_2_3.js.title: Common Misconception: Incomplete Velocity
note_physics_1_2_3.js.text: Velocity must include direction. Writing 'velocity = 10 m/s' without direction is incomplete — this describes speed, not velocity. Correct form: '10 m/s to the right' or '10 m/s at 30° above horizontal'. In circular motion: constant speed ≠ constant velocity (direction changes) → acceleration exists → force required.

note_physics_1_2_4.js.title: Common Error: Confusing Sin and Cos in Resolution
note_physics_1_2_4.js.text: The component adjacent to angle θ always uses cos θ. The component opposite the angle uses sin θ. If θ is from horizontal: horizontal = F cos θ, vertical = F sin θ. If θ is from vertical: swap them. Always identify which angle is given first.

note_physics_1_2_4.js.title: Exam Tip: Check Your Resolution
note_physics_1_2_4.js.text: After resolving F into Fx and Fy, verify using Pythagoras: Fx² + Fy² = F² (should be true for any angle). This catch prevents sign errors and angle mistakes.

note_physics_1_2_5.js.title: Exam Tip: Stopping Distance Scales with Speed
note_physics_1_2_5.js.text: Thinking distance ∝ v (linear): double speed → double thinking distance. Braking distance ∝ v² (from v² = u² + 2as): double speed → four times braking distance. Speed limits reduce accidents dramatically because braking distance is not proportional to speed — it's proportional to speed squared.

note_physics_1_2_5.js.title: Common Misconception: Speed and Distance
note_physics_1_2_5.js.text: A car at 60 mph does not stop in twice the distance of one at 30 mph due to braking distance scaling with v². Stopping distance increases as v² is much faster than linear increase. Reaction time also increases with tiredness, alcohol, and distractions.

note_physics_1_2_6.js.title: Key Principle: Independence of Motion Components
note_physics_1_2_6.js.text: Horizontal and vertical components of projectile motion are INDEPENDENT. Horizontal: no acceleration (ax = 0), vx = u cos θ = constant. Vertical: acceleration = g downward. Analyse each direction separately using SUVAT.

note_physics_1_2_6.js.title: Air Resistance in Free Fall
note_physics_1_2_6.js.text: A feather and hammer fall at the same rate ONLY in a vacuum. In air, drag force on a feather is enormous compared to its weight. When drag-to-weight ratio is large, objects reach terminal velocity almost instantly and do not accelerate at g. Free-fall at g = 9.81 m/s² applies only to dense/streamlined objects over short distances or in vacuum.

note_physics_1_2_6.js.title: Exam Tip: Sign Convention for g
note_physics_1_2_6.js.text: State your sign convention clearly: 'Taking downward as positive gives a = +9.81 m/s²' or 'taking upward as positive gives a = −9.81 m/s²'. Inconsistent signs are the most common source of error in projectile motion problems.

note_physics_1_2_7.js.title: Exam Tip: Free-Body Diagrams
note_physics_1_2_7.js.text: Free-body diagram shows ONLY forces ON the object of interest, not forces the object exerts on others. Label each force clearly (W = mg, N, f, T, F). For equilibrium: sum of horizontal forces = 0 AND sum of vertical forces = 0.

note_physics_1_2_7.js.title: Exam Tip: Relative Velocity
note_physics_1_2_7.js.text: Relative velocity of A with respect to B = vA − vB. Define positive direction first. Same direction: relative speed = |vA − vB|. Opposite directions: relative speed = |vA| + |vB|. Always draw a diagram labelling the positive direction to avoid sign errors.

note_physics_1_3_0.js.title: Key Formula: F = ma
note_physics_1_3_0.js.text: F must be the NET (resultant) force. To find resultant: add forces in same direction, subtract forces in opposite directions, or resolve into components for 2D problems. Newton's First Law (F = 0 → a = 0) is a special case; Newton's Second Law F = ma is the general form.

note_physics_1_3_0.js.title: Exam Tip: Applying Newton's Laws
note_physics_1_3_0.js.text: State WHICH Newton's law you are applying before writing equations. For Newton's 2nd: identify F as the net force. For Newton's 3rd: pairs must be (1) same magnitude, (2) opposite direction, (3) same force type, (4) on different objects.

note_physics_1_3_1.js.title: Key Distinction: Mass vs Weight
note_physics_1_3_1.js.text: Mass (kg) is scalar, constant everywhere, amount of matter. Weight (N) is vector force, W = mg, depends on gravity. On Earth g = 9.81 N/kg. Same object has different weight on Moon (1.6 g) or Mars (3.7 g) but mass unchanged. Mass measured by balance; weight by spring scale.

note_physics_1_3_1.js.title: Exam Tip: Solving Any Forces Problem
note_physics_1_3_1.js.text: (1) Draw free-body diagram. (2) Choose axes (horizontal/vertical or along/perpendicular to slope). (3) Resolve all forces into components. (4) Apply Newton's 2nd in each direction separately. Never sum forces as scalars if they act in different directions.

note_physics_1_3_2.js.title: Exam Tip: h vs t² Graph
note_physics_1_3_2.js.text: Examiners often ask: 'Why plot h against t² rather than h against t?' Answer: h = ½gt² means h ∝ t² (not t). Plotting h vs t² gives a straight line whose gradient = g/2, making g easy to extract. Plotting h vs t gives a curve and obscures g.

note_physics_1_3_2.js.title: Sources of Error in Freefall
note_physics_1_3_2.js.text: Systematic errors: reaction time delay (use electronic timer to eliminate), air resistance (use dense metal ball, not feather). Random errors: variability in release, timer response fluctuations. Improvements: multiple trials, repeated heights, discard anomalous results, use data logger.

note_physics_1_3_3.js.title: Newton's Third Law: Four Properties
note_physics_1_3_3.js.text: A Newton Third Law pair must satisfy: (1) equal magnitude, (2) opposite direction, (3) same force type (both gravitational, both contact, both electromagnetic), (4) act on DIFFERENT objects. Never on the same object.

note_physics_1_3_3.js.title: Common Misconception: Third Law vs Equilibrium
note_physics_1_3_3.js.text: Weight (Earth on book, gravitational) and normal force N (table on book, contact) are equal and opposite but NOT a Newton third law pair — they are different force types AND both act on the same object. A genuine third law pair: 'Earth pulls book down' ↔ 'book pulls Earth up' (both gravitational, on different objects).

note_physics_1_3_3.js.title: Exam Tip: Three Forces in Equilibrium
note_physics_1_3_3.js.text: For 3 forces in equilibrium — either resolve into components (ΣFx = 0, ΣFy = 0) or draw triangle of forces and apply sine rule. Always define sign convention before resolving. Closed triangle = equilibrium.

note_physics_1_3_4.js.title: Momentum and Impulse
note_physics_1_3_4.js.text: Momentum p = mv (unit kg m/s, vector). Newton's 2nd Law: F = Δp/Δt. Impulse J = FΔt = Δ(mv). Impulse = area under F-t graph. Large force for short time = small force for long time if FΔt is the same.

note_physics_1_3_4.js.title: Exam Tip: Crumple Zones and Airbags
note_physics_1_3_4.js.text: Crumple zone increases collision time Δt. Since Δp is fixed (same collision), longer Δt → smaller average force F = Δp/Δt → reduced injury. Same principle applies to airbags, crash mats, helmets, and catching techniques in sport — all increase contact time to reduce peak force.

note_physics_1_3_5.js.title: Conservation of Momentum Principle
note_physics_1_3_5.js.text: In a closed system (no net external force): total momentum before = total momentum after. m₁u₁ + m₂u₂ = m₁v₁ + m₂v₂. Always choose a positive direction and apply it consistently; use negative for opposite direction motion.

note_physics_1_3_5.js.title: Exam Tip: Identifying Collision Types
note_physics_1_3_5.js.text: Momentum is ALWAYS conserved (elastic or inelastic), provided no net external force. Kinetic energy is conserved ONLY in elastic collisions. Identify by: (1) calculate total p before/after (always equal), (2) calculate total KE — if equal → elastic; if KE decreases → inelastic. In perfectly inelastic collision, use v = m₁u₁/(m₁+m₂).
